title: 2012 NFL season

infobox.value: was the 93rd season of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 47th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. It began on Wednesday, September 5, 2012, with the defending [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI") champion [New York Giants](/wiki/2012_New_York_Giants_season "2012 New York Giants season") falling to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2012_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2012 Dallas Cowboys season") in the 2012 NFL [Kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), and ended with [Super Bowl XLVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVII "Super Bowl XLVII"), the league's championship game, on Sunday, February 3, 2013, at the [Mercedes-Benz Superdome](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Superdome "Mercedes-Benz Superdome") in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), with the [Jim Harbaugh](/wiki/Jim_Harbaugh "Jim Harbaugh")-coached [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2012_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2012 San Francisco 49ers season") facing the [John Harbaugh](/wiki/John_Harbaugh "John Harbaugh")-coached [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2012_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2012 Baltimore Ravens season"). The Ravens won the game 34–31, which marked the first time two brothers were head coaches for opposing teams in the championship game.

The **2012 NFL season** was the 93rd season of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") (NFL) and the 47th of the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl") era. It began on Wednesday, September 5, 2012, with the defending [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI") champion [New York Giants](/wiki/2012_New_York_Giants_season "2012 New York Giants season") falling to the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2012_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2012 Dallas Cowboys season") in the 2012 NFL [Kickoff game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game") at [MetLife Stadium](/wiki/MetLife_Stadium "MetLife Stadium"), and ended with [Super Bowl XLVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVII "Super Bowl XLVII"), the league's championship game, on Sunday, February 3, 2013, at the [Mercedes-Benz Superdome](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Superdome "Mercedes-Benz Superdome") in [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), with the [Jim Harbaugh](/wiki/Jim_Harbaugh "Jim Harbaugh")-coached [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2012_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2012 San Francisco 49ers season") facing the [John Harbaugh](/wiki/John_Harbaugh "John Harbaugh")-coached [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/2012_Baltimore_Ravens_season "2012 Baltimore Ravens season"). The Ravens won the game 34–31, which marked the first time two brothers were head coaches for opposing teams in the championship game.

## Referee labor dispute

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Referee labor dispute")]

Main article: [2012 NFL referee lockout](/wiki/2012_NFL_referee_lockout "2012 NFL referee lockout")

In 2005, the NFL and [NFL Referees Association](/wiki/National_Football_League_Referees_Association "National Football League Referees Association") (NFLRA) agreed to a contract that would last through the 2011 season. In 2011, the officials' union had planned to use a contract clause to reopen negotiations a year early, but this failed to occur due to the [2011 NFL lockout](/wiki/2011_NFL_lockout "2011 NFL lockout").[[1]](#cite_note-a-1)

By June 2012, the league and the officials' union had not yet come to terms on a new [collective bargaining](/wiki/Collective_bargaining "Collective bargaining") agreement, thus failing to resolve the labor dispute. The main issues between the union and the league were changes to the retirement plan, salaries, and personnel. On June 4, 2012, the NFL announced it would begin hiring replacement officials.[[1]](#cite_note-a-1)[[2]](#cite_note-LATimesRefDispute-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

On September 26, 2012, an agreement was reached to end the lockout after increasing criticism of the NFL and the performance of the replacement officials.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) The contentious nature of the replacement officials' decision at the end of the [Green Bay Packers–Seattle Seahawks game](/wiki/2012_Packers%E2%80%93Seahawks_officiating_controversy "2012 Packers–Seahawks officiating controversy") two days earlier was widely considered to have been the tipping point that finally led to an agreement.[[6]](#cite_note-6) NFL Commissioner [Roger Goodell](/wiki/Roger_Goodell "Roger Goodell") acknowledged that the game "may have pushed the parties further along" in negotiations.[[7]](#cite_note-ovation-7)

## Player movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Player movement")]

The 2012 NFL League year and trading period began at 4pm EST on March 13 2012, which marked the start of the league's free agency period. The per-team salary cap was set at US$120,600,000, marginally increased from US$120,000,000 the previous year.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

### Free agency

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Free agency")]

Notable players to change teams during free agency included:

* Quarterbacks [Jason Campbell](/wiki/Jason_Campbell "Jason Campbell") (Oakland to Chicago), [Matt Flynn](/wiki/Matt_Flynn_(American_football) "Matt Flynn (American football)") (Green Bay to Seattle), [Chad Henne](/wiki/Chad_Henne "Chad Henne") (Miami to Jacksonville), [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") (Indianapolis to Denver) and [Kyle Orton](/wiki/Kyle_Orton "Kyle Orton") (Kansas City to Dallas)
* Running backs [Cedric Benson](/wiki/Cedric_Benson "Cedric Benson") (Cincinnati to Green Bay), [Michael Bush](/wiki/Michael_Bush "Michael Bush") (Oakland to Chicago) and [BenJarvus Green-Ellis](/wiki/BenJarvus_Green-Ellis "BenJarvus Green-Ellis") (New England to Cincinnati)
* Fullbacks [Le'Ron McClain](/wiki/Le%27Ron_McClain "Le'Ron McClain") (Kansas City to San Diego) and [Mike Tolbert](/wiki/Mike_Tolbert "Mike Tolbert") (San Diego to Carolina)

## Referee change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Referee change")]

[Bill Vinovich](/wiki/Bill_Vinovich "Bill Vinovich") returned to the field as a substitute referee, working several games during the season. He was originally promoted to referee in 2004, but had to leave the field in 2007 because of a heart condition. During his recovery, he served as a replay official.

## Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The following rule changes have been approved by the competition committee for the 2012 season:

* The Replay Booth can initiate replay reviews on turnover plays at any time during the game, similar to a change made in the [2011 season](/wiki/2011_NFL_season "2011 NFL season") regarding booth reviews on scoring plays outside of the final 2:00 of the game or in overtime. The penalty for throwing a challenge flag immediately after such "unchallengable" plays was also modified: in addition to the 15-yard unsportsmanlike conduct penalty, the Replay Booth will automatically rule that the call will stand without initiating a replay review.[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) This part of the rule was repealed prior to the 2013 season.
* Instant Replay is also expanded to include the following situations
  + A Ruling of "runner out of Bounds" when there is a Fumble and a recovery following that fumble
  + A Ruling of "incomplete Forward Pass" when there is a Backward Pass and a recovery following the backward pass[[19]](#cite_note-19)
* The penalty for 12 men on the field (not in the huddle) is changed from a live-ball foul to a dead-ball foul, with the whistle being blown if the defense has 12 men on the field and the "snap is imminent".
* Adding anyone who is subject to a crack-back block to the list of defenseless players.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
* Last names on uniforms can now include [generational suffixes](/wiki/Suffix_(name) "Suffix (name)") such as Roman numerals (in the case of [Robert Griffin III](/wiki/Robert_Griffin_III "Robert Griffin III")), Junior (Jr.), and Senior (Sr.) designations.[[21]](#cite_note-21)
* Officials no longer could wear white knickers with their uniforms. The full length black pants with a white stripe down the side, worn for cold weather games since 2006, became mandatory for all games.
* The overtime rules in the playoffs (adopted for the [2010 season](/wiki/2010_NFL_season "2010 NFL season")) would be extended to the pre-season and regular season. Instead of a straight sudden death, the game will not immediately end if the team that receives the ball first scores a field goal on its first possession (they can still win the game if they score a touchdown and the game will end if the defense scores a safety). Instead, the other team gets a possession. If the second team on offense then scores a touchdown, it is declared the winner. If the score is tied after both teams had a possession, whether both teams kicked a field goal or neither team scored, then it goes to sudden death. If the score remains tied at the end of overtime, the game ends in a tie.
  + The first regular season game that the new overtime rules were used was a Week 1 contest between the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") and the [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings"). Minnesota took the opening kickoff of overtime and scored on a field goal, then stopped Jacksonville on fourth down.
  + The first regular season game where both teams scored a field goal on their respective first possessions of overtime was the Week 11 game between the Jaguars and the [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans"). The Texans then won the game, becoming the first NFL team to score twice in overtime under the new format.

### Other changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Other changes")]

On August 30, 2012, the owners and [NFLPA](/wiki/NFLPA "NFLPA") agreed to the following changes regarding player movement:[[22]](#cite_note-22)

* The trade deadline has been set as the Tuesday following week 8 of the season. Previously, the trade deadline was the Tuesday following week 6. The deadline was moved back another two days to November 1 due to potential complications regarding [Hurricane Sandy](/wiki/Hurricane_Sandy "Hurricane Sandy") as league offices were closed due to the storm.[[23]](#cite_note-23)
* Teams may designate one player who had been placed on [injured reserve](/wiki/Injured_reserve "Injured reserve") prior to the start of the season to return to the 53-man roster later in the season and play. That player is eligible to return to practice after week 6 and to play after week 8.

## Regular season

### Late doubleheader kickoff time change

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Late doubleheader kickoff time change")]

The league announced on June 28 that all late Sunday doubleheader games will be moved ten minutes later from 4:15 p.m. [ET](/wiki/Eastern_Time_Zone "Eastern Time Zone") to 4:25 p.m. Late games broadcast on the single game network will still remain at 4:05 p.m. The league cited 44 early games from the 2009 to 2011 seasons in which part of the audience had to be switched immediately to the kickoff of their home team's doubleheader game, and thus miss the end of the first game.[[24]](#cite_note-24) The late Dallas Thanksgiving game's kickoff time was also moved from 4:15 p.m. ET to 4:30 p.m. ET. The 4:15 p.m. late doubleheader kickoff time dates back to the 1998 season when the NFL moved it from 4:05 p.m. for the same reason.

### Other highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Other highlights")]

Highlights of the 2012 schedule include:

* **[NFL Kickoff Game](/wiki/National_Football_League_Kickoff_game "National Football League Kickoff game")**: The 2012 regular season began on Wednesday, September 5, as the defending [Super Bowl XLVI](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVI "Super Bowl XLVI") champion [New York Giants](/wiki/2012_New_York_Giants_season "2012 New York Giants season") hosted the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2012_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2012 Dallas Cowboys season"), and the Cowboys beat the Giants 24–17. The game was moved from its usual Thursday slot to avoid conflict with the last day of the [Democratic National Convention](/wiki/2012_Democratic_National_Convention "2012 Democratic National Convention").
* **More [Thursday night games](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")**: On February 3, 2012, commissioner [Roger Goodell](/wiki/Roger_Goodell "Roger Goodell") announced that the number of Thursday night games on the [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") will increase from eight to 13 games from weeks 2 through 15, excluding Week 12 (the Thanksgiving night game will now air on [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football")).[[26]](#cite_note-26) This will ensure that every team will have at least one prime time game.[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* **[International Series](/wiki/NFL_International_Series "NFL International Series")**: The 2012 International Series game featured the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2012_St._Louis_Rams_season "2012 St. Louis Rams season") hosting the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2012_New_England_Patriots_season "2012 New England Patriots season") on October 28, at 1:00 p.m. EDT (5:00 p.m. [GMT](/wiki/Greenwich_Mean_Time "Greenwich Mean Time")) on [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS"), at the permanent International Series home—[Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium") in [London, England](/wiki/London,_England "London, England").[[28]](#cite_note-2012_to_2014-28) The Patriots won the game 45–7. Though the league had originally promised to add a second game in Ireland,[[29]](#cite_note-29) Scotland[[30]](#cite_note-30) or Wales[[31]](#cite_note-31) beginning in 2012,[[32]](#cite_note-ap20111024-32) the league canceled the game, citing the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics") in London as a conflict (the league canceled a second International Series game in 2010 as well, citing the ongoing negotiations of the collective bargaining agreement). This was to be the first of three consecutive International Series appearances for the Rams, but the Rams announced they would no longer take part in the 2013 and 2014 editions due to fan backlash in St. Louis;[[33]](#cite_note-33) beginning in 2013, the [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") will serve as the permanent International Series tenant instead.[[34]](#cite_note-34)
* **[Redskins Rule](/wiki/Redskins_Rule "Redskins Rule")**: The last [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2012_Washington_Redskins_season "2012 Washington Redskins season") home game before the [2012 presidential election](/wiki/2012_United_States_presidential_election "2012 United States presidential election") took place on November 4 against the [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/2012_Carolina_Panthers_season "2012 Carolina Panthers season"). According to the "Redskins Rule", because the Redskins lost that game 21–13, the incumbent president was forecast to lose his bid for re-election on Election Day. In the end, the Redskins Rule failed to come to fruition.
* **[Thanksgiving Day games](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day")**: Three games were played on Thursday, November 22. The two traditional Thanksgiving games saw the [Houston Texans](/wiki/2012_Houston_Texans_season "2012 Houston Texans season") defeat the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2012_Detroit_Lions_season "2012 Detroit Lions season"), 34–31 in overtime; followed by the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/2012_Washington_Redskins_season "2012 Washington Redskins season") defeating their longtime division rivals, the [Dallas Cowboys](/wiki/2012_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2012 Dallas Cowboys season"), 38–31. In the prime-time Thanksgiving game, which for the first time aired on [NBC](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"), the [New England Patriots](/wiki/2012_New_England_Patriots_season "2012 New England Patriots season") defeated the [New York Jets](/wiki/2012_New_York_Jets_season "2012 New York Jets season") 49–19. The Patriots scored 35 points in the second quarter, including a return of the "[Butt Fumble](/wiki/Butt_Fumble "Butt Fumble")" for a touchdown. Prior to 2012, the prime-time Thanksgiving game aired on [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network").
* **[Bills Toronto Series](/wiki/Bills_Toronto_Series "Bills Toronto Series")**. The fifth and, under current contract, final regular-season game of the series, which saw the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/2012_Buffalo_Bills_season "2012 Buffalo Bills season") play in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto")'s [Rogers Centre](/wiki/Rogers_Centre "Rogers Centre"), featured the Bills hosting the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/2012_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2012 Seattle Seahawks season") on December 16. The Seahawks defeated the Bills 50–17. Due to a re-emergence of late-season attendance problems at [Ralph Wilson Stadium](/wiki/Ralph_Wilson_Stadium "Ralph Wilson Stadium"), the Toronto Series will return to its original timing after the end of the [2012 CFL season](/wiki/2012_CFL_season "2012 CFL season"). Although a preseason game was originally going to be played in 2012 as part of the series, it was canceled due to a lack of available dates at the Rogers Centre.[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* **[Christmas Eve](/wiki/NFL_on_Christmas_Eve "NFL on Christmas Eve")**: Christmas Eve fell on a Monday in 2012. Since the NFL usually avoids scheduling games on the night of Christmas Eve, the ESPN Monday Night game for that week was instead played on Saturday, December 22, between the [Atlanta Falcons](/wiki/2012_Atlanta_Falcons_season "2012 Atlanta Falcons season") and the [Detroit Lions](/wiki/2012_Detroit_Lions_season "2012 Detroit Lions season"). It was the only Saturday game played during the 2012 regular season and the Falcons won the game 31–18. This also prevented a conflict with ESPN also covering the college football bowl game, the [Hawaii Bowl](/wiki/Hawaii_Bowl "Hawaii Bowl") which was played on December 24.
* **[Playoffs](/wiki/National_Football_League_playoffs "National Football League playoffs")**: The last regular season games were held on Sunday, December 30. The playoffs started on Saturday, January 5, 2013 and the conference championship games were held on Sunday, January 20; the NFC Championship was played at 3:00 p.m. EST on Fox, and the AFC Championship followed at 6:30 p.m. EST on CBS. [Super Bowl XLVII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLVII "Super Bowl XLVII"), the league's championship game, was on February 3 at the [Mercedes-Benz Superdome](/wiki/Mercedes-Benz_Superdome "Mercedes-Benz Superdome") in [New Orleans, Louisiana](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans"), and was televised on CBS with kickoff around 6:20 p.m. EST. Pre-game programming began that morning with *[CBS News Sunday Morning](/wiki/CBS_News_Sunday_Morning "CBS News Sunday Morning")* and *[Face the Nation](/wiki/Face_the_Nation "Face the Nation")* being Super Bowl-centric followed by "official" pregame programming.

The [Pro Football Hall of Fame Game](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame_Game "Pro Football Hall of Fame Game") was played August 5 and featured a match-up between the [Arizona Cardinals](/wiki/2012_Arizona_Cardinals_season "2012 Arizona Cardinals season") and the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/2012_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2012 New Orleans Saints season"). The Saints last appeared in the game in [2007](/wiki/2007_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2007 New Orleans Saints season"); former Saints offensive tackle [Willie Roaf](/wiki/Willie_Roaf "Willie Roaf") was inducted as part of the Hall of Fame ceremonies. The Cardinals played there for the first time since [1986](/wiki/1986_St._Louis_Cardinals_(NFL)_season "1986 St. Louis Cardinals (NFL) season"), when the franchise was still located in St. Louis. As with the originally announced 2011 game, this matchup broke from the game's usual tradition of featuring two teams from opposing conferences (the 2011 Hall of Fame Game would've featured the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/2011_Chicago_Bears_season "2011 Chicago Bears season") and [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2011_St._Louis_Rams_season "2011 St. Louis Rams season"), but the game was canceled due to the [2011 NFL lockout](/wiki/2011_NFL_lockout "2011 NFL lockout")).[[36]](#cite_note-36) The game, normally airing on [NBC](/wiki/NBC "NBC"), instead aired this season on [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network") due to NBC airing the [2012 Summer Olympics](/wiki/2012_Summer_Olympics "2012 Summer Olympics"), as it had done in 2007. The Saints defeated the Cardinals, 17–10. The remainder of the 2012 preseason matchups were revealed on April 4.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

The November 11 game between the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/2012_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2012 San Francisco 49ers season") and the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/2012_St._Louis_Rams_season "2012 St. Louis Rams season") ended in a rare [tied game](/wiki/List_of_NFL_tied_games "List of NFL tied games"), with each team scoring 24 points, none in the overtime period. Prior to this, the last tie game had been in [2008](/wiki/2008_NFL_season#Tie_game "2008 NFL season").[[38]](#cite_note-38)

The [2013 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2013_Pro_Bowl "2013 Pro Bowl") was held in Hawaii on January 27, 2013, after New Orleans was briefly considered as a site.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Originally, the NFL delayed announcing a date or venue for the game, and even considered eliminating the game altogether due to the NFL's displeasure with the quality of play in the [2012 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2012_Pro_Bowl "2012 Pro Bowl").[[40]](#cite_note-40)

* **Week 16**: The [San Francisco](/wiki/2012_San_Francisco_49ers_season "2012 San Francisco 49ers season")–[Seattle](/wiki/2012_Seattle_Seahawks_season "2012 Seattle Seahawks season") game, originally scheduled at 4:25 p.m. EST on [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox"), was flexed into the 8:20 p.m. EST time slot on [NBC Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football"). * **Week 17**: The [Dallas](/wiki/2012_Dallas_Cowboys_season "2012 Dallas Cowboys season")–[Washington](/wiki/2012_Washington_Redskins_season "2012 Washington Redskins season") game, originally scheduled at 1:00 p.m. EST, was selected as the final Sunday Night Football game, which for the second consecutive season decided the [NFC East](/wiki/NFC_East "NFC East") division champion. The [Miami](/wiki/2012_Miami_Dolphins_season "2012 Miami Dolphins season")–New England and [Green Bay](/wiki/2012_Green_Bay_Packers_season "2012 Green Bay Packers season")–[Minnesota](/wiki/2012_Minnesota_Vikings_season "2012 Minnesota Vikings season") games were moved from 1:00 p.m. EST to 4:25 p.m. EST.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

## Postseason

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Postseason")]

Main article: [2012–13 NFL playoffs](/wiki/2012%E2%80%9313_NFL_playoffs "2012–13 NFL playoffs")

Within each conference, the four division winners and the top two [non-division winners with the best overall regular season records)](/wiki/Wild_card_(sports) "Wild card (sports)") qualified for the playoffs. The four division winners are [seeded](/wiki/Seed_(sports) "Seed (sports)") 1–4 based on their overall won-lost-tied record, and the wild card teams are seeded 5–6. The NFL does not use a fixed bracket playoff system, and there are no restrictions regarding teams from the same division matching up in any round. In the first round, dubbed the *wild-card playoffs* or *wild-card weekend*, the third-seeded division winner hosts the sixth-seed wild card, and the fourth seed hosts the fifth. The 1 and 2 seeds from each conference received a [first-round bye](/wiki/Bye_(sports) "Bye (sports)"). In the second round, the *divisional playoffs*, the number 1 seed hosts the worst-surviving seed from the first round (seed 4, 5, or 6), while the number 2 seed will play the other team (seed 3, 4, or 5). The two surviving teams from each conference's divisional playoff games met in the respective AFC and NFC Conference Championship games, hosted by the higher seed. Although the [Super Bowl](/wiki/Super_Bowl "Super Bowl"), the championship round of the playoffs, is played at a neutral site, the designated home team is based on an annual rotation by conference.[[45]](#cite_note-yahoo-57)

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### Saints bounty scandal

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Saints bounty scandal")]

Main article: [New Orleans Saints bounty scandal](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints_bounty_scandal "New Orleans Saints bounty scandal")

In 2012, the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") were discovered to have run a "slush fund" under former defensive coordinator [Gregg Williams](/wiki/Gregg_Williams "Gregg Williams"), that paid out bonuses, called "bounties", to purposely injure offensive players that the Saints were playing against. The system was known to have operated during Williams's time in [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills")[[46]](#cite_note-58) and [Washington](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins").[[47]](#cite_note-59) Rumors started in 2009 during the Saints [Super Bowl XLIV](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XLIV "Super Bowl XLIV") run in the 2009 NFC Championship game against the Vikings, where the Saints defense was allegedly trying to hurt Vikings quarterback [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre").[[48]](#cite_note-60)[[49]](#cite_note-61) Other than the Vikings, the Saints also allegedly targeted [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears")[[50]](#cite_note-62) and [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers")[[51]](#cite_note-63) players, and the program became even more notorious in the 2011 NFL Divisional Playoff Game against the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers"), when filmmaker [Sean Pamphilon](/wiki/Sean_Pamphilon "Sean Pamphilon") released audio tapes of Williams telling his players to injure a select group of 49ers, with one of them being running back [Kendall Hunter](/wiki/Kendall_Hunter "Kendall Hunter"), and to knock him out, as well as going after [Kyle Williams](/wiki/Kyle_Williams_(wide_receiver,_born_1988) "Kyle Williams (wide receiver, born 1988)") because of his history of concussions. Williams also told them to injure [Vernon Davis](/wiki/Vernon_Davis "Vernon Davis")' ankles and [tear](/wiki/Anterior_cruciate_ligament_injury "Anterior cruciate ligament injury") wide receiver [Michael Crabtree](/wiki/Michael_Crabtree "Michael Crabtree")'s [ACL](/wiki/Anterior_cruciate_ligament "Anterior cruciate ligament"). According to Pamphilon, Williams also appeared to put a bounty on quarterback [Alex Smith](/wiki/Alex_Smith "Alex Smith") after he told his men to hit Smith in the chin, "then he rubs his thumb against his index and middle fingers – the cash sign – and says, *I got the first one. I got the first one. Go get it. Go lay that [expletive] out.*"[[52]](#cite_note-64)

Ultimately, Goodell handed down one of the harshest penalties in league history, by suspending Williams indefinitely, head coach [Sean Payton](/wiki/Sean_Payton "Sean Payton") for the entire [2012 season](/wiki/2012_New_Orleans_Saints_season "2012 New Orleans Saints season"), interim head coach [Joe Vitt](/wiki/Joe_Vitt "Joe Vitt") for the first 6 games, and general manager [Mickey Loomis](/wiki/Mickey_Loomis "Mickey Loomis") for 8 games.[[53]](#cite_note-sanction-65) Saints linebacker [Jonathan Vilma](/wiki/Jonathan_Vilma "Jonathan Vilma") was also suspended for the season, as well as defensive linemen [Anthony Hargrove](/wiki/Anthony_Hargrove "Anthony Hargrove") and [Will Smith](/wiki/Will_Smith_(defensive_end) "Will Smith (defensive end)") for 8 and 4 games, respectively. Former Saints and current [Cleveland Browns](/wiki/Cleveland_Browns "Cleveland Browns") linebacker [Scott Fujita](/wiki/Scott_Fujita "Scott Fujita") was also suspended for 3 games.[[54]](#cite_note-66) The player's suspensions were later thrown out on appeal.[[55]](#cite_note-67)

### Chargers Stickum

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Chargers Stickum")]

During the [Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football") game on October 15 between the [San Diego Chargers](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") and the [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos"), officials checked the hands of Chargers players, under the suspicion that players were using [Stickum](/wiki/Stickum "Stickum") or a similar banned adhesive on players' towels to gain a competitive edge.[[56]](#cite_note-Gehlken-68) Chargers' head coach [Norv Turner](/wiki/Norv_Turner "Norv Turner") strenuously denied the accusations.[[56]](#cite_note-Gehlken-68) The towels were revealed to have [Gorilla Gold Grip Enhancer](/wiki/Gorilla_Gold_Grip_Enhancer "Gorilla Gold Grip Enhancer").[[57]](#cite_note-Gorilla_Gold-69) The Chargers were fined $20,000 by the NFL for failing to immediately surrender team towels when requested, but were cleared of illegal substance use.[[58]](#cite_note-70) Gorilla Gold was subsequently banned from use by the NFL.[[57]](#cite_note-Gorilla_Gold-69)

## League averages

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: League averages")]

There were a total of 11,651 points scored during the 2012 NFL regular season. The average points scored among all the teams in the NFL was 22.8 points per game.

The New England Patriots had the highest point differential, scoring an average of 14.1 points more than their opponents. The Kansas City Chiefs had the lowest point differential scoring an average of 13.4 points less than their opponents.

## Records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Records")]

* [Jason Hanson](/wiki/Jason_Hanson "Jason Hanson") set the record for most consecutive seasons with one team. He has been the kicker for the Detroit Lions for 21 seasons.[[59]](#cite_note-71)
* [Ed Reed](/wiki/Ed_Reed "Ed Reed") of the Baltimore Ravens broke [Rod Woodson](/wiki/Rod_Woodson "Rod Woodson")'s record for interception return yardage in a week one game against the Cincinnati Bengals. He is now the all-time leader in interception return yards with 1,541.[[60]](#cite_note-Reed-72)
* [Robert Griffin III](/wiki/Robert_Griffin_III "Robert Griffin III") became the only player in NFL history to pass for 300+ yards and 2 touchdowns without throwing an interception in his first start.[[60]](#cite_note-Reed-72)
* [David Akers](/wiki/David_Akers "David Akers") tied the NFL record for longest field goal (63 yards).[[61]](#cite_note-73)
* [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning") joined [Dan Marino](/wiki/Dan_Marino "Dan Marino") and [Brett Favre](/wiki/Brett_Favre "Brett Favre") as the only players to throw at least 400 touchdowns.[[62]](#cite_note-74)
* Week 1 set a record for being the highest scoring opening week in NFL history. The new record of 791 points is 3 points higher than the 2002 record. Five teams scored more than 40 points, also the most in NFL history.[[63]](#cite_note-75)
* NFL sets record with 20 teams at 1–1.[[64]](#cite_note-76)
* [Drew Brees](/wiki/Drew_Brees "Drew Brees")' record streak of consecutive games with 300 plus yards passing ended at 9 games in a week 3 loss to the Kansas City Chiefs. His streak began in week 10 of the 2011 season.[[65]](#cite_note-77)[[66]](#cite_note-78)
* [Danny Amendola](/wiki/Danny_Amendola "Danny Amendola") of the St. Louis Rams had 12 receptions in the first half of the week two game vs. Washington to tie the NFL record for most receptions in the first half set by the Indianapolis Colts' [Reggie Wayne](/wiki/Reggie_Wayne "Reggie Wayne") in 2007.[[67]](#cite_note-79)
* The [Tennessee Titans](/wiki/Tennessee_Titans "Tennessee Titans") scored a record five touchdowns of 60-plus yards in one game (1 punt return, 2 pass plays, 1 kickoff return, 1 fumble return) against the Detroit Lions in week 3.[[68]](#cite_note-boxscore-80)[[69]](#cite_note-Lion-81)
* The week 3 game between the [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") and [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") produced an NFL record 13 first downs via penalties. Of the 13, New England was awarded 8 and Baltimore 5.[[70]](#cite_note-82)
* In week 4, Rams rookie kicker [Greg Zuerlein](/wiki/Greg_Zuerlein_(American_football) "Greg Zuerlein (American football)") became the first player in league history to make kicks from 50-plus and 60-plus yards in the same game.[[71]](#cite_note-83)
* Drew Brees' record of 48 consecutive games with a touchdown pass in week 5 vs. [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego_Chargers "San Diego Chargers") (the first team Brees played for) broke [Johnny Unitas](/wiki/Johnny_Unitas "Johnny Unitas")' record that had stood since 1960.[[72]](#cite_note-84) The streak ended at 54 games in week thirteen against the Atlanta Falcons.[[73]](#cite_note-85)[[74]](#cite_note-86)
* [Charles Tillman](/wiki/Charles_Tillman "Charles Tillman") and [Lance Briggs](/wiki/Lance_Briggs "Lance Briggs") set a new record by intercepting a pass for a touchdown in both Week 4 and Week 5. They became the first pair of teammates in NFL history to each return an interception for a touchdown in consecutive games.[[75]](#cite_note-87)
* In week five the [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers") became the first team in NFL history with 300 yards passing and 300 yards rushing vs. the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills").[[76]](#cite_note-88)
* [Chris Johnson](/wiki/Chris_Johnson_(running_back) "Chris Johnson (running back)") of the Tennessee Titans ran for an 83-yard touchdown in the first quarter of the Titans' win over the Buffalo Bills to become the first player in NFL history with four 80-plus yard touchdown runs in a career.[[77]](#cite_note-89)
* The [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") set a record of seven consecutive games without holding a lead, previously set by the [1929 Buffalo Bisons](/wiki/1929_Buffalo_Bisons_season "1929 Buffalo Bisons season").[[78]](#cite_note-90)
* Week 8: New England gained over 350 yards of total offense for the 17th straight game, breaking an NFL record set by the Rams in 1999–2000.[[79]](#cite_note-91)
* By allowing 530 yards by the Broncos in week eight, the [New Orleans Saints](/wiki/New_Orleans_Saints "New Orleans Saints") became the first team to allow 400-plus yards in seven games in a row since 1950, which is as far back as STATS LLC can search its NFL database.[[80]](#cite_note-92) Record ended at ten games through week 11.[[81]](#cite_note-93)
* In week 8, [Jason Witten](/wiki/Jason_Witten "Jason Witten") of the Cowboys caught 18 passes against the Giants. This set a new NFL record for catches in a game by a tight end, and was the third most in a single game by any player in NFL history.[[82]](#cite_note-94)
* Through week eight Peyton Manning has increased his record of most regular season games with 300 plus yards passing to 68 games.[[83]](#cite_note-95)
* [Andrew Luck](/wiki/Andrew_Luck "Andrew Luck") broke the NFL's single-game rookie record when he threw for 433 yards to lead the Indianapolis Colts past the Miami Dolphins.[[84]](#cite_note-96)
* Chicago became the first team in NFL history to record a touchdown pass, a touchdown run, an interception return for a touchdown, and a blocked kick/punt for a score in the same quarter in their week 9 game against the Tennessee Titans.[[85]](#cite_note-Bears_Week_9-97)
* Charles Tillman became the first player in the NFL to force four fumbles in one game since the stat became tracked in 1991.[[85]](#cite_note-Bears_Week_9-97)
* [Doug Martin](/wiki/Doug_Martin_(running_back) "Doug Martin (running back)") became the first player in league history to record touchdown runs of 70-plus, 65-plus, and 45-plus yards in a single game.[[86]](#cite_note-98) He joined Denver's [Mike Anderson](/wiki/Mike_Anderson_(running_back) "Mike Anderson (running back)") as the only players in league history with at least 250 rushing yards and four touchdowns in a game.[[87]](#cite_note-99)
* [Jacoby Jones](/wiki/Jacoby_Jones "Jacoby Jones") of the Ravens became the first player in league history with two career kickoff returns of at least 105 yards. He returned one for 108 yards against Dallas; he followed it up a few weeks later when he returned one for 105 yards against Oakland, simultaneously tying the record for longest kickoff return on the former return.[[88]](#cite_note-100)[[89]](#cite_note-101)
* Andrew Luck set the rookie record with six games of at least 300 yards passing.[[90]](#cite_note-102)
* [Leon Washington](/wiki/Leon_Washington "Leon Washington") of the Seahawks returned a kickoff for a touchdown for the eighth time to tie the NFL career record.[[91]](#cite_note-103)
* [Calvin Johnson](/wiki/Calvin_Johnson "Calvin Johnson") of the Lions broke the NFL season receiving yards record against the Falcons on December 22.[[92]](#cite_note-104)
* [Blair Walsh](/wiki/Blair_Walsh "Blair Walsh") broke the NFL single season record for most field goals made in a season of over 50 yards on December 23 against the Texans.[[93]](#cite_note-105)
* Placekicker [Kai Forbath](/wiki/Kai_Forbath "Kai Forbath") of the Redskins set a new NFL record with 17 consecutive field goals to start a career.[[94]](#cite_note-106)
* Andrew Luck broke the rookie record for passing yards in a season on December 23.[[95]](#cite_note-107)
* [Jason Witten](/wiki/Jason_Witten "Jason Witten") broke the NFL single season record for catches by a tight end on December 23.[[96]](#cite_note-108)
* [Adrian Peterson](/wiki/Adrian_Peterson "Adrian Peterson") became the seventh player in NFL history to rush for 2,000 yards in a single season by rushing for 199 yards in Week 17, bringing his season total to 2,097 rushing yards. This also brings him just nine yards short of breaking [Eric Dickerson](/wiki/Eric_Dickerson "Eric Dickerson")'s record set in 1984 and gives him the all-time second best single season record for rushing yards.[[97]](#cite_note-109)
* [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson") tied [Peyton Manning](/wiki/Peyton_Manning "Peyton Manning")'s rookie record for touchdown passes in a single season with 26.[[98]](#cite_note-110)
* The [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") gained an NFL record 444 first downs.[[99]](#cite_note-111)
* The 2012 regular season set the NFL record for total points scored in a season with 11,651; the 22.8 points-per-game for each team is also the highest since the [AFL-NFL merger](/wiki/AFL-NFL_merger "AFL-NFL merger").[[100]](#cite_note-112)
* The [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") set the NFL record for most playoff losses with 27.
* The [Minnesota Vikings](/wiki/Minnesota_Vikings "Minnesota Vikings") broke the NFL record for most road playoff losses with 16.
* The [New England Patriots](/wiki/New_England_Patriots "New England Patriots") tied the [St. Louis Rams](/wiki/St._Louis_Rams "St. Louis Rams")' record set from 1999 to 2001 for the most consecutive seasons scoring 500 or more points with three.
* [Robert Griffin III](/wiki/Robert_Griffin_III "Robert Griffin III") set a passer rating of 102.4, to break [Ben Roethlisberger](/wiki/Ben_Roethlisberger "Ben Roethlisberger")'s record for the highest rating by a rookie.
* Most home playoff games won: 20, [San Francisco 49ers](/wiki/San_Francisco_49ers "San Francisco 49ers")
* [Colin Kaepernick](/wiki/Colin_Kaepernick "Colin Kaepernick") set record for most rushing yards by a quarterback in a single game, with 181 yards.[[101]](#cite_note-113)
* [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady") set the record for most playoff games won with 17.[[102]](#cite_note-114)
* Most conference championship games played starting quarterback (tie): 7, [Tom Brady](/wiki/Tom_Brady "Tom Brady")
* [Russell Wilson](/wiki/Russell_Wilson "Russell Wilson") set a rookie record for passing yards in a playoff game : 385.
* [Joe Flacco](/wiki/Joe_Flacco "Joe Flacco") tied [Joe Montana](/wiki/Joe_Montana "Joe Montana")'s record for most touchdown passes (11) without an interception in a postseason

## Uniforms

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Uniforms")]

* Nike became the official uniform provider for the NFL, succeeding [Reebok](/wiki/Reebok "Reebok"), which had a decade-long partnership in that capacity. On April 3, Nike unveiled the new uniforms for all 32 teams.[[158]](#cite_note-170)[[159]](#cite_note-171) Cosmetically, the new jerseys did not show drastic differences save for the aforementioned changes to the [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks")' uniforms.[[160]](#cite_note-172)
* The [Carolina Panthers](/wiki/Carolina_Panthers "Carolina Panthers") unveiled an updated logo and word mark in late January 2012,[[161]](#cite_note-173) though it has been reported that the actual uniforms will not be altered at the present time.[[162]](#cite_note-174)
* The [Denver Broncos](/wiki/Denver_Broncos "Denver Broncos") switched their primary home jersey color from navy blue to orange. The orange jerseys that served as the team's alternate colored jersey from 2002 to 2011 became the primary home jersey, while the navy blue jerseys that served as the team's primary home jersey from 1997 to 2011 switched to the alternate designation. The change was made due to overwhelming popularity with fans, who clamored for the team to return to wearing orange at home, which was the team's predominant home jersey color from 1962 to 1996.[[163]](#cite_note-175)
* The [Houston Texans](/wiki/Houston_Texans "Houston Texans") celebrated 10 years as a franchise by wearing an anniversary patch throughout the season.[[164]](#cite_note-176)
* The [Jacksonville Jaguars](/wiki/Jacksonville_Jaguars "Jacksonville Jaguars") switched their primary home jersey color from teal to black, beginning with their Week 5 (October 7, 2012) game against the [Chicago Bears](/wiki/Chicago_Bears "Chicago Bears").[[165]](#cite_note-177)
* The [Kansas City Chiefs](/wiki/Kansas_City_Chiefs "Kansas City Chiefs") debuted captains' patches to their uniforms for the first time in the Week 10 (November 12, 2012) game against Pittsburgh. The patches would remain through the rest of the season but were removed in 2013.
* The [Pittsburgh Steelers](/wiki/Pittsburgh_Steelers "Pittsburgh Steelers") unveiled a new throwback uniform in April 2012, which was worn in games against the [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") and the [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens").[[166]](#cite_note-178) The uniforms, based on the [1934 Pittsburgh Pirates](/wiki/1934_Pittsburgh_Pirates_(NFL)_season "1934 Pittsburgh Pirates (NFL) season") (the predecessors to the Steelers), feature broad black and Aztec gold horizontal stripes across the jerseys and socks, reminiscent of [bumblebees](/wiki/Bumblebee "Bumblebee") and prison uniforms.[[167]](#cite_note-179)
* The [Seattle Seahawks](/wiki/Seattle_Seahawks "Seattle Seahawks") replaced Seahawk blue with College Navy as the color of their primary uniforms. Their new uniforms consist of a navy blue jersey as their primary home jersey, a white jersey as their primary away jersey, and a wolf grey jersey as an alternate. The Seahawks have three pairs of pants: navy blue with action green feather trim, white with navy blue feather trim, and wolf grey with navy blue feather trim. Each pair of pants, as well as the collar of the jerseys, feature 12 feathers, in honor of the fans, who refer to themselves as the '12th Man'. The Seahawks also tweaked their logo, by changing the lower left stripe from light blue to grey.[[168]](#cite_note-180)
* The [Washington Redskins](/wiki/Washington_Redskins "Washington Redskins") wore throwback uniforms to celebrate their 80th anniversary[[169]](#cite_note-181)[[170]](#cite_note-182) in their game against the Panthers.[[171]](#cite_note-183)
* The [Baltimore Ravens](/wiki/Baltimore_Ravens "Baltimore Ravens") dedicated their season to former owner and founder [Art Modell](/wiki/Art_Modell "Art Modell"), who died on September 6, 2012.[[172]](#cite_note-184) On Week 1, all team members wore an "Art" decal on their helmets, and for the rest of their season, they wore an "Art" patch on the left side of their jerseys.
* During the [2012 Pro Bowl](/wiki/2012_Pro_Bowl "2012 Pro Bowl"), [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") debuted new team color specific cleats and new team specific Vapor Jet gloves. Similar to gloves worn at the collegiate level, the Vapor Jet gloves feature individual team colors and team logos on the glove palms.[[173]](#cite_note-185)[[174]](#cite_note-186)
* The Carolina Panthers' new jersey has the phrase "KEEP POUNDING" inside the collar which commemorates former Panther [Sam Mills](/wiki/Sam_Mills "Sam Mills")' battle with cancer.
* Nike's new jerseys also introduced what the company calls a "body-contoured fit".[[175]](#cite_note-WSJ20121005-187) However, several players, primarily heavier players such as offensive linemen, reported that the new, tighter-fitting uniforms made them "look fat".[[175]](#cite_note-WSJ20121005-187)
* Introduced in Week 5 after the end of the referee lockout, the new referee uniform used the NFL's "Orbit" font that the league uses in its branding and marketing.[[176]](#cite_note-188)
* During Weeks 14 and 15, all teams wore a commemorative patch celebrating the 50th anniversary of the [Pro Football Hall of Fame](/wiki/Pro_Football_Hall_of_Fame "Pro Football Hall of Fame").[[177]](#cite_note-189)

## Media changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=2012_NFL_season&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Media changes")]

This was the seventh season under the television contracts with the league's television partners. On December 14, 2011, the NFL announced that it had extended all of its broadcasting contracts through 2022. There were some changes to the contracts, with the most immediate change in 2012 being the [Thanksgiving night game](/wiki/NFL_on_Thanksgiving_Day "NFL on Thanksgiving Day") being promoted from [NFL Network](/wiki/NFL_Network "NFL Network")'s *[Thursday Night Football](/wiki/Thursday_Night_Football "Thursday Night Football")* package to NBC's *[Sunday Night Football](/wiki/NBC_Sunday_Night_Football "NBC Sunday Night Football")* package.[[178]](#cite_note-Newsday_NewTVContract-190) [CBS](/wiki/NFL_on_CBS "NFL on CBS") continued to primarily televise AFC afternoon away games, [Fox](/wiki/NFL_on_Fox "NFL on Fox") airing NFC afternoon away games, and [ESPN](/wiki/ESPN "ESPN") broadcasting *[Monday Night Football](/wiki/Monday_Night_Football "Monday Night Football")* games.

ESPN made a cut to its *Monday Night Football* broadcasts, removing [Ron Jaworski](/wiki/Ron_Jaworski "Ron Jaworski") from the broadcast booth and moving to a two-man announcing crew; [Mike Tirico](/wiki/Mike_Tirico "Mike Tirico") will continue on play-by-play while [Jon Gruden](/wiki/Jon_Gruden "Jon Gruden") continues as the sole color commentator.[[179]](#cite_note-191) One year prior, the network had switched from two sideline reporters to one.

The 2013 Pro Bowl was televised by NBC; although the game was normally assigned to the Super Bowl's broadcaster, CBS declined.[[180]](#cite_note-192)

The NFL authorized a new rule loosening the league's [blackout](/wiki/Blackout_(broadcasting) "Blackout (broadcasting)") restrictions during the 2012 offseason. For the first time in NFL history, the new rule no longer requires a stadium to be sold out to televise a game; instead, teams were allowed to set a benchmark anywhere from 85 to 100 percent of the stadium's non-premium seats. Any seats sold beyond that benchmark will be subject to heavier [revenue sharing](/wiki/Revenue_sharing "Revenue sharing").[[181]](#cite_note-193) Four clubs opted to set the lower TV threshold: the Miami Dolphins, the Minnesota Vikings, the Oakland Raiders, and the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.[[182]](#cite_note-194) At least four other teams expressly refused to lower their threshold; one case, that of the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills"), was particularly controversial, as Buffalo congressman [Brian Higgins](/wiki/Brian_Higgins "Brian Higgins") had lobbied for the loosening of the blackout restrictions only for the Bills to rebuff his efforts, saying such a move would threaten the team's revenue.[[183]](#cite_note-195)[[184]](#cite_note-196)
